1: Saul orders Jonathan and his servants to kill David. The command that the anointed king be put to death by the existing power structure is the pattern fulfilled in the Sanhedrin's decision against Jesus: “it is better for you that one man die for the people than that the whole nation perish” (John 11:50). In both cases the established authority perceives the anointed as a threat and issues a death order. Jonathan's refusal to obey the death order parallels those in the passion narrative who resist the crowd's momentum (Pilate's attempts to release Jesus, Nicodemus's protest in John 7:51).
2: Jonathan warns David — “be on guard tomorrow morning; stay in a secret place and hide” — the covenant friend protecting the anointed from the lethal threat. The disciples warning Paul of plots (Acts 9:24; 1 Samuel 20:3) and the angel warning Joseph to flee to Egypt (Matthew 2:13) follow the same structural pattern of covenant loyalty providing protective intelligence. The one who loves the anointed risks his own position to secure the anointed's survival — Jonathan risks Saul's wrath as the disciples risk the crowd's anger.
3: Jonathan positions himself as the intermediary between David and Saul — standing near his father in the field to speak on David's behalf. The mediating position — between the threatening authority and the threatened anointed — is the structure of Christ's high priestly intercession: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25). Jonathan places himself between the executioner and the condemned, which is structurally what Christ's death accomplishes: standing between the just condemnation of sinners and the sinners themselves.
4: Jonathan's defense of David before Saul: “the king must not wrong his servant David, because he has not wronged you” — an appeal to the innocence of the accused before the hostile judge. Peter's defense of Jesus before the crowd (Acts 3:13-14: “you handed over and rejected him in Pilate's presence, even though he decided to release him”) and Pilate's own repeated declaration (John 18:38; 1 Samuel 19:4,6: “I find no basis for a charge against him”) establish Jesus's innocence through exactly this courtroom-advocacy pattern that Jonathan models before Saul.
5: “He risked his life and struck down the Philistine, and YHWH won a great victory for all Israel.” Jonathan's appeal rests on David's prior covenant service: the act of self-giving for the nation's sake establishes the claim to covenant protection in return. Christ's argument before the Father rests on his own prior act of self-giving: “by his own blood he entered once for all into the holy places, having obtained an eternal redemption” (Hebrews 9:12). The intercessor whose plea rests on his own sacrificial act on behalf of the ones he intercedes for — this is the structure both Jonathan and Christ embody.
6: Saul swears an oath: “As YHWH lives, David shall not be put to death.” The temporary reconciliation accomplished through Jonathan's advocacy restores David to Saul's presence — but it will not last. Christ's intercession, by contrast, is permanently effective: “he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25). Jonathan's successful intercession restores David provisionally; Christ's intercession restores us permanently.
7: Jonathan calls David and brings him back to Saul; David serves before Saul as before. The restoration of the anointed to the covenant community through the advocate's intervention is the pattern of reconciliation theology. Paul in 2 Corinthians 5:18-19: “God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ... not counting their trespasses against them.” David's return to service before Saul through Jonathan's mediation is the small-scale type of humanity's restoration to service before YHWH through Christ's mediation.
8: War breaks out again; David fights and routes the Philistines. David's ongoing military service — fighting the nation's enemies while under threat from within his own court — parallels Christ's redemptive work accomplished amid the hostility of those he came to save. The pattern: the anointed's greatest victories for the covenant community occur precisely during the period when the covenant community's leadership is most hostile to him.
9: A harmful spirit from YHWH came upon Saul as David played the lyre. The recurrence of the evil spirit despite Jonathan's successful intercession marks the structural limitation of human mediation: Jonathan cannot permanently solve what is a spiritual-covenantal problem with Saul's heart. Only the mediator whose intercession is grounded in his own atonement (Christ) achieves permanent reconciliation; the mediator whose intercession is merely verbal (Jonathan) buys time but cannot secure permanent peace.
10: Saul hurls the spear at David; David dodges and it sticks in the wall. The assassination attempt at close range, in the context of service, is the structure of the betrayal scenario — the deadly attack from the insider position. Judas at the table (Luke 22:21: “the hand of him who betrays me is with mine on the table”) and the arrest in Gethsemane represent this same “attack during service” pattern. David's escape from the spear is YHWH's preservation of the anointed; Christ's survival until “his hour had come” (John 2:4; 1 Samuel 7:30; 1 Samuel 8:20: they tried to seize him but no one laid a hand on him because his hour had not yet come) is the same divine timing.
11: Saul sends agents to watch David's house through the night and kill him in the morning. Michal warns David: “if you don't save yourself tonight, tomorrow you'll be dead.” The nighttime watch of the anointed's dwelling with intent to kill at dawn is the structural background for the disciples watching in Gethsemane (with the opposite dynamic: the watch fails to protect, and at dawn the arrest occurs). Both involve a nighttime transition from safety to mortal danger for YHWH's anointed.
12: Michal lowered David through a window, and he fled. The window-escape of the anointed to preserve his life from a lethal authority is the OT pattern that recurs in Acts 9:25 — Paul lowered in a basket through the city wall at Damascus to escape the plot against his life. Both escapes preserve a covenant servant whose mission is not yet complete; both are accomplished through the agency of a protector who risks their own position. The anointed's work must be completed before his life is taken; premature death is prevented by YHWH's providence working through human instruments.
13: Michal places a household idol in the bed with a goat-hair cushion at the head to deceive the messengers. The image of a sleeping figure used to buy the anointed time is a deception that protects David — similar to the deception of the guards at the tomb (Matthew 28:12-15: the priests paid the soldiers to say the disciples stole the body while they slept). In both cases a constructed false narrative of “presence” (David apparently sick in bed; Jesus apparently still in the tomb) is used to give the anointed time — one to escape, one to fulfill his resurrection mission before official opposition could organize.
14: Michal tells the messengers David is ill. The false report delays the execution order. The lie that protects the anointed is Michal's personal risk — she will face Saul's anger for this deception. Christ's disciples risk themselves to protect his mission: Joseph of Arimathea going to Pilate to secure the body (Mark 15:43: “taking courage”), Nicodemus bringing spices for burial. The covenant friends who put themselves at risk to serve the anointed at moments of crisis form the supporting cast of both David's and Jesus's story.
15: Saul's intensified command — “bring him in his bed, that I may kill him” — demonstrates how opposition to the anointed escalates when initial attempts fail. The intensification pattern is consistent in the gospels: the Pharisees' initial criticism escalates to plotting (John 11:53: from that day they made plans to put him to death), intensifies further to conspiracy (Matthew 26:3-4), and culminates in the trial. The failure to eliminate the anointed at each stage drives the opposition to more extreme measures.
16: The messengers enter and find only the idol in the bed. The discovery of the empty place where the anointed was expected to be dead is the anti-type of the empty tomb. The women came to the tomb and found it empty (Mark 16:4-6: “he has risen; he is not here”); Saul's agents came to David's bed and found only a substitute image. In both cases the anointed is gone when his enemies expected to find him; in both cases the escape confounds and outrages the opposition.
17: Saul demands: “Why have you deceived me?” Michal replies she had no choice — David threatened her life. The pressure exerted by hostile authority on those who protected the anointed is consistent: after the crucifixion, the disciples feared the Jewish leaders (John 20:19: they were gathered with doors locked for fear of the Jews). Those who show covenant loyalty to Christ must often navigate the hostility of powers threatened by his survival and witness.
18: David flees to Samuel at Ramah and tells him everything Saul has done. The anointed king fleeing to the prophet who anointed him — bringing his full situation and seeking prophetic counsel — is the pattern of retreat to the source of commissioning under pressure. Christ at Gethsemane brings his full situation to the Father in prayer (Luke 22:41-44: “Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me”) — the complete openness between the anointed and the one who sent him, even in extremity. Samuel and David together at Naioth is the prophetic community as a sanctuary for the persecuted anointed.
19: Word reaches Saul: “David is at Naioth in Ramah.” The discovery of the anointed's location by hostile intelligence is a recurring pattern. The Magi's information leading Herod to send soldiers to Bethlehem (Matthew 2:16), Judas guiding the arrest party to Gethsemane (John 18:2: “Judas, who betrayed him, also knew the place”) — in each case informants reveal where the anointed can be found. The difference: David escapes Naioth; Jesus willingly accepts the arrest when his hour arrives.
20: Saul's messengers arrive at Naioth and the Spirit of God comes upon them; they prophesy instead of arresting David. The Spirit preventing the arrest of the anointed by transforming his would-be captors into involuntary worshipers is the divine protection pattern fulfilled in Jesus's pre-Passion protection. John 7:45-46: the temple officers sent to arrest Jesus return empty-handed — “no one ever spoke the way this man does.” The divine authority that surrounded the anointed repeatedly prevented premature arrest until “the hour had come” for his voluntary self-giving.
21: Three successive groups of Saul's messengers come to Naioth; all prophesy; none arrest David. The threefold repetition of failed attempts to capture the anointed — each attempt met with the same divine intervention — establishes the pattern of YHWH's sovereign protection. Acts 2:24 applies this principle to the resurrection: “it was not possible for him to be held” by death. The same impossible-to-hold principle governs David's preservation at Naioth — three groups sent, none succeed — and Christ's preservation until his voluntary death.
22: Saul goes himself to Ramah, inquiring at the great cistern at Sechu. The escalation from sending agents to coming personally — when all other attempts have failed — mirrors the escalation in the Passion narrative: from sending servants to arrest Jesus (Mark 12:2-5), to sending soldiers (John 18:3), to the high priest himself presiding over the interrogation (Mark 14:53). In both cases the hostile authority's final personal investment in eliminating the anointed is the climactic stage of the pursuit.
23: As Saul walks toward Naioth the Spirit of God comes upon him and he prophesies all the way there, until he reaches Naioth. The king who comes to destroy the anointed is transformed by the Spirit into a prophet glorifying YHWH before he arrives. Paul's Damascus road conversion is the NT counterpart: the one sent to arrest and destroy the followers of Christ is transformed by the Spirit into Christ's most effective apostle before he arrives at his destination. In both cases YHWH converts the chief persecutor through a Spirit-encounter on the way.
24: Saul strips off his garments and prophesies before Samuel, lying naked all day and all night. The total incapacitation of the king who sought to kill the anointed — stripped, prostrate, prophesying against his own will — is the pattern of the enemies of Christ being overcome not by force but by divine power. Philippians 2:10-11: “at the name of Jesus every knee will bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.” Saul's involuntary prostration before Samuel (the prophet of the anointed) is the small-scale type of the eschatological involuntary confession of every enemy of the anointed King.
